title: How the body communicates through symptom language
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The body speaks before the mind knows what to say

I spent years trying to think my way out of my own body. I'd sit in therapy, talk about my childhood, name the feelings, connect the dots. And still, I'd wake up with my jaw clenched so tight my teeth ached. Still, my stomach would knot up before meetings. Still, I'd get that tightness in my chest that no amount of rationalizing could untangle.

Here's the thing. Your body has been talking to you this whole time. You just weren't trained to listen.

We live in a culture that prizes the mind. We're told to think positive, to reframe our thoughts, to cognitive-behavioral our way out of discomfort. But the nervous system doesn't give a damn about your positive affirmations when it's stuck in a survival response. It's been scanning for danger since before you had words. And it's still scanning.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. His research showed that traumatic experiences get stored not just in memory but in the physical tissues, the nervous system, the gut. You can talk about the past until you're blue in the face, but if your body hasn't discharged that survival energy, it's still carrying it. Right?

I remember a client who came to me with chronic migraines. She'd been to neurologists, tried medications, done the elimination diets. Nothing worked. Then we started paying attention to when the migraines hit. Always after she'd been in situations where she felt powerless. Always after she'd swallowed her anger to keep the peace. Her body was screaming what her mouth couldn't say.

The symptom was the message.

Think about it. That tightness in your shoulders? That's not random. That's your body bracing for impact. That chronic fatigue? That's not laziness. That's your system running on empty because it's been in survival mode for so long that rest feels dangerous. That IBS flare-up? That's your gut-brain axis saying "I can't digest this situation."

Polyvagal theory and the language of the body

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, gave us a framework for understanding this. He showed that our nervous system has three distinct pathways: the ventral vagal (social engagement), the sympathetic (fight or flight), and the dorsal vagal (freeze, collapse, shutdown). These aren't just theoretical categories. They're physiological states that show up in your body as specific sensations.

When you're in ventral vagal, you feel safe. Your face is relaxed, your voice has range, your gut works properly, you can connect with people. When you're sympathetic, you feel that wired, on-edge sensation. Your heart races, your breathing gets shallow, your muscles tense. When you're dorsal, you feel numb, heavy, disconnected, like you're underwater or watching life from behind glass.

These states aren't bad. They're adaptive. Your body is doing exactly what it evolved to do. But when you've experienced complex trauma, these systems get stuck. You might live in a chronic sympathetic state, always bracing, never settling. Or you might collapse into dorsal, feeling empty and disconnected from your own life. Or you might ping-pong between them, never finding that safe middle ground.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Deb Dana, who wrote this book, breaks down how to actually work with these states in real time. It's not just theory. It's a map for reading your own nervous system.

I started tracking my own body states after reading her work. I noticed that before every phone call with my mother, my throat would tighten. That was my dorsal vagal kicking in, preparing me to freeze and fawn. Before every difficult conversation, my hands would get cold. That's blood moving to my core, a sympathetic response preparing for action. These weren't random. They were predictable patterns my body had learned decades ago.

And here's the wild part. Once I could name the state, I could start to shift it. Not by forcing myself to relax. That never works. But by acknowledging what was happening and giving my body what it actually needed. Sometimes that meant a long exhale. Sometimes it meant putting a hand on my chest. Sometimes it meant getting up and walking across the room to complete that fight-or-flight circuit.

Stay with me here. This is not about fixing yourself. This is about learning a new language. Your body has been speaking in symptoms for years. It's time you started listening.

The symptom as a signal, not an enemy

We're conditioned to see symptoms as problems to be eliminated. Take this pill. Do this protocol. Suppress this feeling. But what if the symptom is actually a signal? What if it's information your body is trying to deliver?

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how animals in the wild shake off trauma after a life-threatening event. They don't ruminate. They don't talk about it. They complete the survival response physically. Then they go back to grazing. Humans, on the other hand, get stuck. We suppress the shaking. We hold the breath. We tighten the muscles. And that incomplete survival energy turns into chronic symptoms.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Levine walks through how trauma gets trapped in the body and how to release it safely. It's not about reliving the trauma. It's about completing the response that got interrupted.

I had a moment of this myself a few years ago. I was in a yoga class, and the teacher asked us to lie in savasana. My body started trembling. Not shivering. Trembling. My legs shook. My arms vibrated. My teeth chattered. At first, I was embarrassed. I thought I was doing something wrong. Then I remembered Levine's work. I let it happen. I didn't fight it. After about five minutes, it stopped. And I felt this wave of calm wash over me. That was old survival energy finally discharging. My body had been holding that tension for decades, waiting for permission to let go.

Here's what I want you to understand. Your chronic pain, your digestive issues, your fatigue, your insomnia, your tension headaches. These are not your enemies. They are your body's attempt to communicate with you. They are the only language your nervous system has when words fail.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports the parasympathetic nervous system, the part that helps you rest and digest. I take it at night, and it helps my body remember how to settle. But I'll be honest. No supplement can replace the work of actually listening to what your body is saying. It's a support, not a cure.

How to start translating your body's language

You don't need to become a somatic expert overnight. You just need to start paying attention. Here's a simple practice that changed everything for me.

Several times a day, pause and scan your body. Not to fix anything. Just to notice. What's happening in your jaw? Your shoulders? Your stomach? Your hands? Your breath? Don't judge it. Don't try to change it. Just notice. Give it a label. "My jaw is tight." "My stomach is knotted." "My chest feels hollow." That's it.

Over time, you'll start to see patterns. You'll notice that your shoulders tense up before you check your email. You'll notice that your stomach drops when you think about that one person. You'll notice that your breath gets shallow when you're about to say no to something. These are not random. These are your body's way of telling you what's happening beneath the surface.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It's a simple device that makes your exhale longer than your inhale, which activates the vagus nerve and tells your body it's safe. I use it when I'm feeling wired and can't settle. It's not a magic fix, but it helps bridge the gap between knowing and doing.

The goal is not to eliminate symptoms. The goal is to understand what they're telling you. When you start listening, the symptoms often change. They might get quieter. They might shift location. They might intensify before they release. That's okay. That's the process.

I've been doing this work for years, and I still get surprised. Just last week, I noticed a tightness in my chest that I'd been carrying for days. I sat with it, breathed into it, asked it what it needed. And I heard, clear as day, "I'm afraid of being seen." That wasn't a thought I'd had consciously. But my body knew. It always knows.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - A practical guide to understanding your nervous system states and working with them in real time.
  • Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link) - A device designed to extend the exhale, supporting vagal nerve activation and calm.
  • Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link) - A highly absorbable magnesium form that supports relaxation and nervous system health.
